label: Esther — Orit Ge’ez Primary — Complete
summary_plain: Esther tells of the covenant people’s survival in exile under the Persian empire. Mordekhai refuses to bow to Haman. Haman plots to destroy all the Yehudim in every province of the kingdom. Esther, who is queen, risks her life to approach the king without being summoned. She reveals Haman’s plot. The decree is reversed. The Yehudim are given the right to defend themselves. Haman is hanged on the gallows he built for Mordekhai. The days of Purim are established as a memorial — days of feasting and gladness, of sending portions to one another and gifts to the poor.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the Orit Ge’ez as the primary witness for Esther, supplemented by DSS fragments (4QEsther). The Orit Ge’ez text preserves a longer version that includes explicit prayers to YHWH by Mordekhai and Esther — prayers absent from the Masoretic Hebrew text. The Masoretic Esther is the only book in the Hebrew canon that never mentions the name of YHWH. The Orit Ge’ez corrects this omission: YHWH is named directly in the prayers. The Greek Septuagint additions also include these prayers, confirming that the shorter Masoretic text is a truncation. The Qumran evidence is fragmentary but significant: Esther was the last canonical book to be attested at Qumran, and its absence from certain Qumran collections may reflect sectarian discomfort with Persian-era political theology rather than rejection of the text itself.

key_terms.definition: The festival established to commemorate the deliverance of the Yehudim from Haman’s plot. The name comes from ‘pur’ (lot) — Haman cast lots to determine the day of destruction. The lot intended for death became the day of deliverance.

A Yehudi of the tribe of Binyamin living in Shushan (Susa), the Persian capital. He raised Esther and refused to bow to Haman. His refusal triggered the crisis and his faithfulness secured the deliverance. Anglicized as Mordecai.

The Ethiopic scriptural tradition preserved by the Beta Yisra’el and the Ethiopian church. For Esther, the Orit Ge’ez preserves prayers to YHWH that are absent from the Masoretic Hebrew text, making it the more complete primary witness.

fill_blank.prompt: For if you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance will arise for the Yehudim from another place, but you and your father’s house will ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: For if you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance will arise for the Yehudim from another place, but you and your father’s house will perish.

fill_blank.prompt: Go, gather all the Yehudim in Shushan, and fast for me. Do not eat or drink for ______ days, night or day.
fill_blank.source_quote: Go, gather all the Yehudim to be found in Shushan, and hold a fast on my behalf. Do not eat or drink for three days, night or day.

fill_blank.prompt: The days on which the Yehudim got relief from their enemies, and the month that was turned from sorrow to ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: The days on which the Yehudim got relief from their enemies, and the month that had been turned for them from sorrow to gladness and from mourning into a holiday.

multiple_choice.question: Why did Haman plot to destroy all the Yehudim?
options: Because the Yehudim rebelled against the king
Because Mordekhai refused to bow to him
Because Esther insulted him at a banquet
Because the Yehudim refused to pay taxes
multiple_choice.source_quote: And all the king’s servants who were at the king’s gate bowed down and paid homage to Haman. But Mordekhai did not bow down or pay homage.

multiple_choice.question: What did Mordekhai say would happen if Esther remained silent?
options: The Yehudim would be destroyed permanently
Relief and deliverance would arise from another place, but Esther’s house would perish
The king would protect them regardless
multiple_choice.source_quote: For if you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance will arise for the Yehudim from another place, but you and your father’s house will perish.

multiple_choice.question: What does the Orit Ge’ez text of Esther preserve that the Masoretic Hebrew text does not?
options: Additional chapters about the Persian wars
Explicit prayers to YHWH by Mordekhai and Esther
A description of the Second Temple
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Orit Ge’ez preserves prayers to YHWH that are absent from the Masoretic Hebrew text.

faq.question: Why does the ACR use the Orit Ge’ez as the primary witness for Esther?
faq.answer: The Masoretic Hebrew text of Esther is the only book in the Hebrew canon that never mentions the name YHWH. Not once. The Orit Ge’ez text preserves explicit prayers by Mordekhai and Esther that name YHWH directly — prayers that the Greek Septuagint additions also confirm. The Masoretic text’s silence about YHWH is not original — it is a truncation. Someone removed the prayers. The Orit Ge’ez, preserved by the Beta Yisra’el in Ethiopia far from the Masoretic editorial tradition, retained what was cut. This is the Great Eraser operating within the canon itself: the name of YHWH was removed from the very text meant to celebrate his deliverance. The ACR restores the primary witness by reading from the text that preserves the name.

faq.question: How does Purim relate to the Great Eraser framework?
faq.answer: Purim is the festival of failed erasure. Haman’s decree was total: destroy, kill, and annihilate all the Yehudim — young and old, women and children — in a single day (3:13). This is the template for every subsequent attempt at covenant-people erasure: the Seleucid persecution, the Roman destruction, the medieval expulsions, the Inquisition, the transatlantic slave trade, the colonial renaming campaigns. Each follows Haman’s pattern: identify the people, issue the decree, set the date. But Purim also records the template for survival: Mordekhai does not bow, Esther approaches the throne, the decree is reversed. The lot (pur) cast for destruction becomes the day of deliverance. The Great Eraser casts lots. YHWH overturns them. Purim is the annual reminder that the erasure will not succeed.

faq.question: Why was Esther the last canonical book attested at Qumran?
faq.answer: Esther fragments are the rarest biblical texts at Qumran. Some scholars note that the Qumran community may have had reservations about Esther because of its Persian court setting and (in the Masoretic version) its silence about YHWH. But the Orit Ge’ez text resolves this: when the prayers to YHWH are included, Esther is fully covenant-aligned. The Qumran sectarians may have known a shorter version that troubled them — or the fragmentary evidence may simply reflect the accidents of preservation. Either way, the Orit Ge’ez witness demonstrates that the original Esther tradition included direct appeals to YHWH, and the Masoretic truncation is the anomaly, not the Ge’ez expansion.
